title: hooks-mux: add HTTP, MCP tool, prompt, and agent handler types
issueSummary: hooks-mux currently routes every hook handler through shell command execution. Add typed handler support for HTTP webhooks, MCP tool calls, LLM prompt evaluation, and agent/subagent execution while preserving legacy command handler behavior.
issueContextNotes: Issue body names new handler modules: handlers/http.ts, handlers/mcp-tool.ts, handlers/prompt.ts, handlers/agent.ts.
Issue body names runner.ts dispatch and plan.ts HandlerRef changes.
Comment notes dependency on #636 for missing event dispatch infrastructure.
Comment notes relation to #576 because mcp_tool must integrate with tool-mux rather than creating a duplicate MCP registry.

A previous closed PR #695 contains useful prior art and implementation notes, but current staging still shows HandlerRef typed only as command/shell and runHandler dispatching unconditionally to runShellHandler.

methodologySummary: ATDD/TDD: establish acceptance and contract tests before production code.
Process hardening: use bounded repair loops and explicit final acceptance gates.
Verification before completion: require fresh command evidence before claiming success.
TDD quality convergence: iterate only on concrete verification or review gaps.

reuseAudit.planningConstraint: Reuse hooks-mux runner, result, merge, timeout, diagnostics, API export, bridge, and MCP/tool registry surfaces. Do not create a parallel registry or broaden into unrelated hook capability issues.

acceptanceCriteria: HandlerRef supports a backward-compatible typed model where omitted type remains command.
runHandler dispatches by handler type and rejects or reports unsupported types deterministically.
Existing shell command handler behavior, timeout behavior, shell selection, priority ordering, async/once behavior, fail-open/fail-closed behavior, and merge semantics are preserved.
HTTP handlers POST normalized hook events, parse UnifiedHookResult JSON, enforce timeout/cancellation, validate URLs, and interpolate only explicitly allowed environment variables into headers.
MCP tool handlers use the existing tool-mux/MCP registry direction or a narrow injectable executor seam and do not create a second registry.
Prompt and agent handlers use bounded executor seams with timeout/depth/max-turn controls and deterministic unavailable behavior when no executor is configured.
Agent-platform AMUX bridge preserves typed handler config when it currently narrows or drops fields.
Docs in docs/agent-stack/hooks/missing-capabilities.md accurately reflect implemented core support and any deferred live platform integration.

Contract tests cover legacy command behavior, each new handler type, unsupported types, timeouts, fail-open/fail-closed behavior, HTTP safety, MCP registry reuse assumptions, prompt/agent bounds, and bridge pass-through where changed.
nonGoals: Do not implement #638 decision type semantics in this process.
Do not implement #639 matcher, async, once, or per-hook config gaps beyond preserving existing behavior.
Do not implement full #576 tool-mux registry work unless a narrow compatibility seam is required for this issue.
Do not add real external network dependencies to tests.
Do not modify unrelated source files or process artifacts.
qualityGates: Reuse-audit findings are rendered before implementation.
Dependency readiness for #636 and #576 is explicit before contract design.
Contract tests are written before production code and initially fail for the actual gaps.
All target handler types have deterministic tests with no real external services.
HTTP safety checks cover URL validation and allowedEnvVars-only interpolation.
MCP implementation does not duplicate the tool registry.
Prompt and agent handlers are bounded and cannot recurse or run indefinitely.
Fresh verification commands pass or have exact environment-only blockers.
Final review approves backwards compatibility, security controls, docs accuracy, and residual dependency notes.
